messages.text: DVT readiness review scheduled for Friday 3pm. Each workstream lead needs a 5-min status update: open blockers, critical test results, and supply chain risks.
Please also include a go/no-go recommendation for each workstream. We need to decide on Friday whether DVT build starts on schedule or slips.
Drivetrain is go pending the thermal interface fix. @U003 when will we have validation data on the new TIM?
Testing the Fujipoly XR-Um today. Results by Thursday AM.
Supply chain is conditional go. The FPGA situation is resolved (switched to XC7A50T) but the LIDAR lead time is tight - 8 weeks with no float.
Chassis is conditional go. The snap-fit redesign is done and tested. Magnesium housing decision is pending vendor quotes - won't affect DVT timeline either way.
Sensors are go. LIDAR calibration issue resolved with thermal compensation. Camera FOV accepted for DVT with PVT action item logged.
Firmware is go. FPGA timing resolved. Bootloader v2.1 ready. Motor control loop validated. Memory usage is tight but manageable.
Power systems is go. BMS SOC accuracy fixed with 16-bit ADC. Driver board thermal addressed with via stitching. Battery cycling on track.
Good. Looks like we're at conditional go across the board. I'll compile the readiness matrix and distribute before Friday. Blockers: TIM validation, LIDAR float.
Pre-DVT safety review findings: 3 open items. (1) E-stop wiring needs redundant path, (2) bumper force sensor calibration drift, (3) motor controller watchdog timeout too long at 500ms.
These are all blockers for DVT entry. @U004 own the e-stop and watchdog. @U005 own the bumper sensor.
E-stop: adding second contactor in series. Hardware change, 2 days. Watchdog: reducing to 50ms. Firmware change, 1 hour. Both done by Wednesday.
Bumper sensor: the drift is temperature-related. Adding runtime calibration offset. Fix ready by Tuesday.
Team - great progress this sprint. Customer demo is scheduled for 6 weeks after DVT build starts. Need the core navigation loop running by then.
Nav loop is on track. @U006 and @U005 are integrating the LIDAR-based SLAM with the motor controller this week. Basic warehouse navigation demo should be ready.
Perfect. The customer specifically wants to see obstacle avoidance and pallet detection. Make sure those are in the demo script.
Updated project timeline on Confluence. DVT build: weeks 12-14. Field test: weeks 15-18. Customer demo: week 18. PVT: weeks 22-26.
Test lab schedule is booked solid weeks 12-14. I've reserved the thermal chamber and vibration table for DVT validation. First come first served for the EMC chamber.
All hands reminder: design freeze for DVT is next Friday. Any changes after that go through the ECO process. No exceptions.
DVT readiness matrix distributed. Summary: 5 workstreams at GO, 2 at CONDITIONAL GO (chassis mass, sensor FOV). No NO-GO items. Review Friday as planned.
Good position. The two conditional items have clear paths to resolution. I'm comfortable proceeding.
Agreed. Let's confirm GO on Friday and start DVT material ordering Monday. The team has done excellent work getting here.
DVT test infrastructure status: thermal chamber calibrated, vibe table load-tested, EMC pre-scan done. We're ready for units.
Also booked the IP testing lab for week 14. They need 2 units and 3 days for the full IP54 suite.
Updated the test schedule in Confluence. Critical path is thermal cycling - takes 2 full weeks.
Design documentation audit: 85% of subsystems have complete design docs. Missing: power sequencing spec, sensor fusion algorithm description, FPGA register map.
Power sequencing spec draft is ready. Reviewing today, publishing tomorrow.
Sensor fusion doc is 70% done. Will complete by EOW.
FPGA register map auto-generated from the HDL. Just need to add descriptions. Done by Wednesday.
Target: 100% documentation coverage before DVT entry. Please prioritize.
Weekly metrics: 23 open action items (down from 31 last week), 0 critical blockers, 2 medium risks (LIDAR float, mag housing timeline).
Board update prep: I need a 1-page executive summary by Thursday. @U011 can you compile from the readiness matrix?
Will do. Including: DVT readiness status, cost vs budget, timeline risks, and customer demo plan.
EMC pre-scan results are clean at conducted emissions. Radiated is borderline at 230MHz - the motor PWM switching frequency harmonic. Adding ferrite bead.
Reliability prediction complete: MTBF estimated at 8,200 hours. Customer spec is 5,000 hours. 64% margin.
Standup notes: Chassis rev D released. Drivetrain endurance test passing. Thermal fix validated. Power BMS updated. Sensors SLAM qualified. Firmware v0.9 ready. Supply chain material ordered.
Best status update we've had all quarter. Team is executing well. Keep the momentum through DVT.
Agreed. I'll share these results with the board. This team is ahead of the industry average pace for a program of this complexity.
Updated FMEA with latest test results. RPN for 3 failure modes reduced below threshold. 2 remain above: battery cell failure and motor bearing seizure.
Risk register updated: 4 green, 2 yellow (LIDAR float, mag housing timeline), 0 red. Best position since program inception.
Test automation framework v2 deployed. 85% of DVT tests can run unattended overnight. Remaining 15% require manual fixture setup.
Reminder: design freeze is tomorrow at 5pm. After that all changes require ECO. Submit any pending changes now.
Pull-out force on snap fit measured at 12N, spec is 15N min. Third iteration and we're still under spec.
Did you test with the updated rib geometry from rev C? I pushed the STEP file yesterday.
Yes, rev C. The issue is the draft angle on the retention hook - 3deg is too aggressive for PA66-GF30.
What if we go to 1.5deg draft and add a barb feature? Worked on the sensor bracket.
Barb adds undercut complexity for the tool. @U013 can the mold vendor handle that with a side action?
Side action adds 3 weeks to tool lead time and ~$4k. Alternative: lifter pin, but wall thickness needs 2.5mm min.
Current wall is 2.0mm. Going to 2.5 means re-running the FEA for drop test compliance.
I can run the drop sim tonight. What's the impact energy spec - 1.5J at 1m?
Correct, 1.5J free fall from 1m onto concrete per IEC 62368. Corner impact case is usually the limiting factor.
For what it's worth, the sensor bracket passed at 2.2mm wall with the barb. Different load path though.
Talked to the mold vendor. Lifter pin is feasible at 2.5mm wall. They need the updated Parasolid by Friday.
Decision: go with 2.5mm wall + lifter pin. @U008 run drop sim, I'll update the Parasolid. Target: Thursday EOD.
Drop sim results: 2.5mm wall passes at 1.8J - 20% margin above spec. Corner impact stress at 85% of yield.
Good margin. Parasolid updated and uploaded. @U013 please forward to vendor for T1 tooling quote.
Sent. Vendor confirms 6 week lead time from PO. I'll get the quote to Elena for approval by Monday.
Updated mass properties for rev C chassis: 4.73kg. Budget is 4.5kg. We're 230g over.
The 2.5mm wall change adds ~80g. Where else can we pocket? The motor mount plate has material to remove.
Topology optimization on the motor mount showed 150g savings possible. Sending the optimized STEP now.
FYI the new aluminum extrusion supplier quoted 30% lower than current. But we should also consider magnesium for the main housing - 35% weight savings at similar strength.
Magnesium is interesting for the weight target. What's the corrosion protection story? We need IP54.
Micro-arc oxidation coating handles IP54. But the real question is supply chain - mag die casting has longer lead times and fewer qualified vendors.
Let's prototype the housing in magnesium for DVT. If it works, we switch. If not, we stay aluminum and eat the weight penalty. @U013 can you get vendor quotes?
On it. I'll need 2-3 weeks for vendor qualification. This will affect the supply chain timeline if we go mag.
Chassis DVT test plan review meeting moved to Thursday 2pm. Updated invite sent.
Reminder: chassis assembly torque audit is next week. Please have all fastener specs documented in the BOM.
Updated topology-optimized motor mount STEP uploaded. Mass savings: 148g. Total chassis now at 4.58kg - within 2% of the 4.5kg budget.
Good progress. The remaining 80g is in the battery tray. Can we pocket the underside without compromising stiffness?
Running a quick modal analysis to check first resonant freq. Battery tray needs to stay above 200Hz to avoid coupling with the motor vibration.
Modal results: pocketed tray first mode at 245Hz. Safe. Mass with pockets: 4.48kg. Under budget.
Nice work. I'll update the assembly drawing and release to the model vault. Rev D it is.
IP54 ingress test on chassis rev C: dust test passed, water splash test passed all faces. One leak point at the cable gland for motor harness.
Cable gland is the M20 Hummel? We might need the version with double sealing lip.
Correct. The single lip version leaks at 45-degree spray. Double lip version is $2 more per unit. 8 glands per robot.
$16 per unit for IP54 compliance is a no-brainer. Switching to double lip. @U017 please update the BOM.
Vibration test profile for chassis DVT: random vibe 0.5g RMS 10-500Hz, 30 min per axis. Plus sine sweep 5-200Hz at 0.25g for resonance hunting.
The motor excitation is primarily at 150-180Hz range. Make sure the sine sweep has fine resolution through that band - 0.5Hz steps.
Noted. Also adding shock test: half-sine 30g 11ms per IEC 60068-2-27. Test fixturing designed - need 3D printed adapter for shaker table.
I can print the adapter on the Markforged. Send me the STEP and I'll have it ready by Thursday.
Drawing review complete for motor mount rev D. All GD&T callouts verified. Ready for vendor quote.
Vendor confirmed receipt of updated Parasolid. Tool modification quote expected Monday. Lifter pin change adds $1.2k to the tool cost.
Acceptable. Total tool cost is still within budget. @U007 please include in the DVT cost rollup.
Fastener audit results: 23 unique fastener types in chassis assembly. Industry best practice is <15. Can we consolidate?
Agreed, 23 is too many. The M3x8 and M3x10 can merge to M3x10. Same for M4x12 and M4x14. That's -4 types.
Also 5 of those are one-off shoulder screws. Can we redesign those features to use standard hex cap?
3 of the 5 can change. The other 2 need shoulders for bearing preload. That gets us to 16 types. Close enough.
Good. Updated BOM with consolidated fastener list. Also reduces kitting complexity for the assembly line.
Torque audit complete. All 47 fasteners within spec. 3 were at lower bound - tightened to nominal.
The aluminum extrusion vendor is quoting competitively. If we stay aluminum (no mag housing), per-unit cost drops $45 on the chassis alone.
FEA convergence study complete for motor mount. Stress results stable within 2% at current mesh density. No further refinement needed.
Updated the assembly manual for rev D chassis. Added photos for the new snap-fit retention and cable gland installation. Shared on Confluence.
Chassis changes submitted: rev D assembly, 2.5mm wall snap fit, pocketed battery tray, double-lip cable glands. Ready for freeze.
Load testing results are in. Peak torque demand is 3.1Nm, not 2.5Nm as originally spec'd. The additional friction from the new wheel compound is higher than modeled.
3.1Nm at what duty cycle? Continuous or peak? The Maxon EC-i 40 is rated 2.83Nm continuous, 4.2Nm peak.
It's a 15s peak during obstacle climbing. Thermal margin on the motor is the concern - @U003 can you check the thermal derating at 3.1Nm continuous equiv?
Running the thermal model now. At 3.1Nm equiv the winding temp hits 142C - max rated is 155C. Only 13C margin.
That's tight. Options: (1) upsize to EC-i 52, (2) improve thermal path from motor to chassis, (3) reduce friction.
EC-i 52 adds 180g per motor, 720g total for 4 wheels. That blows the mass budget. Option 2 is cheaper.
From power side: 3.1Nm at 200rpm needs ~65W per motor. Current driver board handles 80W max. We're fine on power.
Decision: update torque spec to 3.1Nm. Keep EC-i 40 motor, improve thermal interface between motor housing and chassis heat sink. @U003 own the thermal fix.
Accepted. I'll spec a thermal pad with >3 W/mK between motor flange and chassis mount. Should drop winding temp by 20-25C.
Good. Also updating the gearbox input torque spec. The harmonic drive needs to be re-validated at 3.1Nm input. @U012 please run the lifetime calc.
Hearing intermittent bearing noise from wheel 3 during endurance testing. ~2kHz whine above 150rpm.
Pull it for teardown. Could be preload issue from the last assembly. Check bearing seat concentricity.
Teardown complete. Bearing inner race had 0.02mm step from shaft tolerance. Reshimming fixed it. Updating assembly procedure to add runout check.
Harmonic drive lifetime calc done: 15,000 hours at 3.1Nm input. Spec requires 10,000. Margin is adequate for vendor qualification. Sending validation report to Maxon.
The new motor mount topology-optimized design clears the drivetrain envelope. No interference with gearbox.
Harmonic drive efficiency measurement: 82% at rated load, 78% at 1.5x rated. Spec sheet claims 85%. The 3% loss is in the wave generator bearing.
78% at 1.5x is concerning for the peak torque case. That means 22% of the motor power is heat. Does our thermal budget account for that?
The thermal model assumed 85% efficiency. With 78% we get an additional 4.5W of heat per motor at peak. Need to update the thermal analysis.
The peak is only 15s. Thermal mass should absorb it. @U003 can you confirm the transient thermal response?
Ran the transient: temp rise is 3C over 15s burst. Well within margin. Gearbox steady-state is fine.
Wheel assembly procedure v2 uploaded to Confluence. Key change: bearing preload now set with tooling fixture at 2.5Nm instead of feel. Eliminates the runout issue.
Good. Also add a step for verifying encoder alignment after bearing preload. The last build had 2 misaligned encoders that caused position errors.
Added encoder alignment check with go/no-go gauge. Max allowable offset is 0.05mm radial.
Motor controller auto-calibration routine completed on all 4 motors. Phase resistance and inductance measured within 2% of datasheet values.
What about the back-EMF constant? That's the one that drifts with temperature.
Ke measured at 0.0285 V/rpm vs spec 0.029. Within 2%. I've added temperature compensation in the FOC loop - recalibrates Ke every 60 seconds using the motor temp sensor.
Good. That should eliminate the torque ripple we saw at elevated temps last week.
Drivetrain DVT test matrix finalized: 14 tests across 3 categories (performance, durability, environmental). Shared in Confluence.
Ordered 8 spare harmonic drives for DVT. 2 week lead time. We'll have spares before DVT build starts.
500-hour endurance test on wheels 1&2 complete. Zero failures. Bearing wear within spec. Tire compound shows 0.3mm tread wear - well within the 2mm budget.
Gearbox backlash measurement after 500hr: increased from 6 to 9 arcmin. Spec limit is 15. Trending well.
Good data. We should repeat at 1000hr and 2000hr for the wear trend line. Keep wheels 1&2 running.
Updated the drivetrain design review presentation. Added the torque spec change rationale and the gearbox lifetime analysis. Shared on Confluence.
Wheel 3 back on the endurance test after bearing reshim. Running clean at 500rpm. Will monitor for another 100 hours.
Motor current sense accuracy verified: ±0.5% at full scale. Exceeds the ±1% requirement for FOC control.
All drivetrain changes submitted. Rev D gearbox mount, updated torque spec, thermal interface spec. Ready for design freeze.
FPGA timing closure failing on the motor control path. Setup violation of 1.2ns on the PWM output register. Need to pipeline the output stage.
What clock frequency? If it's the 100MHz domain we can add a register stage without affecting PWM latency.
200MHz motor control clock. Adding a pipeline stage means 5ns additional latency. The control loop can tolerate it - sample rate is 20kHz so 5ns is negligible.
Pipeline stage added. Timing clean now at 200MHz with 0.8ns positive slack. Bitstream rebuilt and tested.
BTW the FPGA we're using (Artix-7 XC7A35T) is showing 12 week lead time from Digikey. Was 4 weeks last month. @U007 @U017 heads up on supply.
12 weeks is a problem. We need 50 units for DVT builds in 8 weeks. Can we get allocation from our distributor?
Checking with Arrow now. They sometimes have stock the web doesn't show. Will update by end of day.
Bootloader v2.1 ready for prototype testing. Added CRC32 validation on firmware images and fallback to golden image on 3 consecutive boot failures.
Does the golden image get updated during OTA? We need to keep it as a known-good fallback.
No, golden image is write-protected in flash. Only the A/B partitions are OTA-updatable. Golden is factory-programmed and never touched.
MCU flash usage: 78% (312KB/400KB). RAM: 85% (170KB/200KB). Getting tight. The navigation stack alone is 45%.
We should profile the nav stack for heap fragmentation. I bet we can reclaim 20KB by switching to fixed-size allocators for the path planner.
Good idea. Also the logging buffer is 32KB - we can compress logs in-place and halve that.
Motor control loop validated at 20kHz update rate. Jitter is <500ns. Meets real-time requirements.
XC7A50T bitstream rebuilt and verified. All timing clean. Extra fabric gives us room for the diagnostic logic we wanted to add later.
How much fabric utilization on the 50T? If we're under 50% we have plenty of room for DVT debug features.
38% LUT utilization, 22% BRAM. Tons of room. Added the ILA debug core - 4K sample depth on the motor control signals.
OTA update stress test: 100 consecutive A/B partition switches. Zero failures. CRC validation catches all injected corruption.
Did you test power loss during flash write? That's the scary case.
Yes, killed power at random during 50 updates. All 50 recovered to the previous good partition. The atomic commit flag works as designed.
Real-time task profiling: motor control ISR 2.1us, navigation loop 1.8ms, sensor fusion 0.9ms. CPU utilization 72% at peak.
72% is on the edge. We should keep it under 80% for margin. Any optimization opportunities?
The SLAM lookup table is in external flash. Moving it to internal SRAM saves 400us per nav cycle. That brings CPU down to 65%.
That eats 48KB of the 200KB RAM though. Given our 85% RAM usage that's tight. What about the compressed log buffer - did we implement that yet?
Not yet. Compressed logs save 16KB. Combined with the fixed-size allocators (20KB savings), we'd free 36KB. Enough for the SLAM table with 8KB to spare.
Let's do all three: compressed logs, fixed allocators, SLAM table in SRAM. I'll start with the log compression.
Firmware v0.9.0 release candidate tagged. Full change log on Confluence. Ready for DVT qualification testing.
Diagnostic telemetry system ready: 32 channels of real-time data over CAN bus. Temperatures, voltages, motor currents, sensor status. 100Hz update rate.
Can we log this to SD card for post-test analysis? The DVT tests will need full data capture.
Yes, SD card logging is included. Binary format with Python decoder script. 8 hours of data fits on a 2GB card at 100Hz.
Perfect for DVT. I'll integrate the decoder into our test automation scripts. CSV output?
Decoder outputs both CSV and parquet. Parquet is 5x smaller and loads faster in pandas.
Fixed-size allocator implementation complete. RAM usage down from 85% to 74%. Log compression next.
CAN bus utilization: 34% at normal operation, 52% during diagnostics mode. Well under the 70% design limit.
Motor control jitter optimization: worst-case ISR latency reduced from 2.1us to 1.4us by moving the ADC trigger to hardware timer compare output.
Firmware v0.9.0 tagged and frozen. Any post-freeze changes go to v0.9.1 branch with ECO.
BMS is reporting 8% SOC error vs coulomb counting. The voltage-based estimation drifts significantly below 20% SOC. Need to calibrate the OCV-SOC lookup table.
Which cell chemistry? The LFP cells have a very flat voltage curve in the 20-80% range which makes voltage-based SOC unreliable.
NMC 21700. The curve isn't flat but the BMS ADC resolution at 12-bit is only 1.2mV/LSB. Need to go to 16-bit or use the coulomb counter as primary below 20%.
The BMS MCU has a 16-bit SAR ADC on channel 2 that's currently unused. I can remap the voltage sense to that channel in firmware. ~2 hours of work.
Do it. That gives us 0.075mV/LSB which is plenty. @U014 please also add the hybrid estimator: coulomb counting primary below 20%, voltage-based above.
Motor driver MOSFET junction temp hit 145C during peak load. Absolute max is 150C. Adding a heatsink to the driver board PCB.
Is that with or without the conformal coating? The coating adds thermal resistance.
Without coating. With coating it'll be worse. Need to either increase copper pour area or add thermal vias under the MOSFET pad.
Updated PCB layout with 25 thermal vias under each MOSFET. Simulated Tj drops to 128C. Sending to vendor for DVT fab run.
Battery cycle test #50 complete. Capacity retention at 97.2%. Datasheet claims 96% at 50 cycles. Validation tracking well for DVT qualification.
Power budget updated: total system draw is 285W peak, 180W nominal. Battery provides 45 min runtime at nominal. Meets the 30 min spec with 50% margin.
BMS firmware v3.2 deployed. Hybrid SOC estimator active: coulomb counting below 20%, voltage-based above. SOC error now <2% across full range.
Verified on bench. SOC tracks within 1.5% of reference coulomb counter. Publish the cal parameters for the production BMS.
Fast charge circuit tested: 0 to 80% in 55 minutes. Spec target is 60 min. Exceeds requirement.
Cell balancing during charge - are we seeing any cell voltage spread at end of charge?
Max cell-to-cell delta at 80% is 12mV. BMS balances during the CV phase. By 100% delta is <5mV. Good.
12mV at 80% is fine. The NMC cells are well matched. We should document the acceptance criteria for incoming cell matching: <15mV spread at 50% SOC.
Power sequencing updated: main rail → MCU → sensors → motor drivers → LIDAR. Each stage has 50ms delay and current limit. Prevents inrush brownout.
Is the FPGA on the MCU rail or its own? It draws 1.2A peak during configuration.
Own rail with dedicated 3.3V regulator. Sequenced after MCU so the SPI bus is ready when FPGA configures.
Perfect. That matches our boot sequence: MCU init → FPGA bitstream load → motor control start.
Battery safety test schedule: nail penetration, overcharge, short circuit, crush. Using 5 sacrificial packs. Test lab booked for week 13.
Ground fault detection circuit validated. Trips at 30mA leakage. Response time: 2ms to motor disconnect. Meets IEC 60204 requirements.
Does it discriminate between ground fault and normal capacitive leakage? The motor cables have ~5mA of capacitive current at PWM frequency.
Yes, the detection uses a DC component filter. PWM capacitive current is AC and gets filtered out. Only DC leakage above 30mA triggers the fault.
Motor controller firmware updated with the 50ms watchdog. Tested E-stop integration: full stop in 180ms from button press. Within the 200ms requirement.
Battery management CAN protocol documentation complete. 12 message IDs covering SOC, cell voltages, temperatures, and fault codes.
Regenerative braking energy capture: recovering 12% of kinetic energy during deceleration. Extends runtime by approximately 8 minutes.
Power system changes frozen: 16-bit ADC, thermal vias, hybrid SOC estimator, ground fault detection. All verified and documented.
LIDAR point cloud has 2.3cm RMS error at 5m range. Spec is <1cm. Recalibrating the intrinsic parameters.
Are you using the factory calibration or our custom procedure? The factory cal drifts with temperature.
Factory cal. Good catch - lab was 28C, calibrated at 22C. The APD gain drifts with temp. Running our thermal compensation routine.
With thermal compensation: 0.8cm RMS at 5m. Meets spec. Adding thermal comp to the boot sequence.
Nice. Make sure the IMU temp sensor feeds into the comp algorithm - it's closer to the LIDAR than the board sensor.
Wheel encoder resolution at 4096 CPR gives 0.087mm per count. Position accuracy requirement is 0.5mm. Fine.
What's the max count rate? At top speed the encoder output is ~200kHz. The MCU timer capture can handle it?
Yes, using quadrature decoder hardware. No CPU overhead.
Front camera FOV measured at 118 deg on the EVT prototype. Spec is 120 min. Edge distortion is higher than expected - barrel at 4.2% vs 3% design target.
Can we correct in software? The ISP has a dewarping block that handles up to 5% barrel.
Yes, tested the ISP dewarp. Corrected image is clean. Effective FOV after correction is 116 deg. Marginal.
116 is below 120 spec. Can we accept 116 or do we need a wider lens? @U010 need a decision.
What's the operational impact of 116 vs 120? If it's just edge coverage we can probably accept for DVT.
4 deg less coverage means 0.35m blind spot at 5m range on each side. Acceptable for warehouse ops, marginal for outdoor. Recommend accept for DVT, fix for PVT.
Agreed. Accept 116 for DVT. Log it as a PVT action item. @U005 please track in the sensor validation matrix.
SLAM validation: position drift is 0.3% of travel distance after 1km loop. Spec is <0.5%. Meeting requirement with margin.
That's with LIDAR only? Or fused with wheel odometry?
Fused: LIDAR + wheel odom + IMU. LIDAR-only is 0.8% drift. The fusion brings it way down.
IMU bias stability is critical for the fusion. Are you running the calibration at every power-on?
Yes, 10-second stationary calibration at boot. Gyro bias converges to <0.01 deg/s within 5 seconds.
Safety LIDAR (rear) installed and calibrated. Detection range: 0.1m to 4m. Response time: <50ms from detection to motor stop command.
50ms is the total including the motor deceleration? Or just the sensor-to-command latency?
Sensor-to-command only. Motor deceleration adds 200ms from full speed. Total stopping distance: 0.35m. Safety zone is set at 0.5m.
The motor controller watchdog now at 50ms (down from 500ms per the safety review). If the safety LIDAR command doesn't arrive in 50ms, motors hard-stop.
Pallet detection hit rate: 94% at 5m range, 98% at 3m. Using 3D point cloud segmentation with RANSAC plane fitting.
What are the false positive rates? A false detection could send the robot into an obstacle.
0.1% false positive rate in the test dataset (10,000 frames). Most false positives are flat walls at oblique angles. Adding geometric constraints to filter those.
94% at 5m is good enough for the customer demo. We approach from 3m anyway for the docking maneuver.
Sensor validation matrix updated. 8 of 12 tests pass. Open items: outdoor LIDAR range, rain performance, vibration immunity. All scheduled for DVT.
Camera image quality in low light: usable down to 50 lux. Warehouse minimum is typically 100 lux. Good.
IMU temperature calibration complete. Gyro bias drift reduced from 0.05 deg/s/C to 0.005 deg/s/C. 10x improvement across -10C to 50C range.
What's the calibration data size? If it fits in the IMU's internal EEPROM we don't need to store it on the MCU side.
256 bytes for the full temp-comp table. IMU EEPROM is 512 bytes with 384 free. It fits. Programming over SPI during factory calibration.
This helps SLAM accuracy significantly. The gyro was the main error source in the sensor fusion. With 10x better stability I expect drift to drop to 0.2%.
Ordered replacement camera lens with wider FOV for PVT evaluation. Should achieve 122 deg with less barrel distortion.
LIDAR dust contamination test: performance degrades above 50mg/m3. Warehouse typical is 5-15mg/m3. Adequate margin. Adding wiper for outdoor use.
Safety LIDAR field-of-view verification: 270 deg horizontal, 30 deg vertical. Meets the ISO 3691-4 requirement for automated guided vehicles.
Sensor subsystem frozen: thermal-compensated LIDAR, safety LIDAR config, IMU temp cal, camera with ISP dewarp. FOV waiver for DVT documented.
DVT build material review. Critical path items and lead times: BLDC motors (6 wk), battery cells (4 wk), LIDAR module (8 wk), custom PCBs (3 wk). DVT build target is 10 weeks out.
LIDAR at 8 weeks is the long pole. I've got a PO ready for Velodyne - just need engineering sign-off on the final part number.
Also need to confirm quantity. @U010 are we building 10 or 15 DVT units?
15 units. 10 for validation, 3 for field testing, 2 spares. Better to order extra now than pay expedite later.
Updated PO for 15x. Total BOM cost per unit: $3,240. 15 units = $48,600. Within the DVT build budget of $55k.
Approved. @U017 please release the PO today. We need those LIDARs moving ASAP given the 8 week lead.
Alert from Arrow: Xilinx Artix-7 XC7A35T allocation is being pulled. New lead time is 16 weeks, up from 12. We need 50 pcs for DVT.
16 weeks is past our DVT build date. Options: (1) expedite at premium, (2) find broker stock, (3) design in alternate FPGA.
Checking brokers. Also the XC7A50T is pin-compatible and in stock - 800 pcs at Arrow. Higher capacity but same footprint. @U006 can firmware use the 50T?
XC7A50T is a drop-in replacement. Same pinout, more logic fabric. Bitstream needs rebuild but no design changes. Go for it.
Decision: switch to XC7A50T. @U017 order 50+10 spare immediately from Arrow stock. @U006 rebuild bitstream.
Magnesium die casting vendor short list: (1) DyCast (domestic, 8wk), (2) Zhongshan (China, 5wk + 3wk ship), (3) MagForm (Mexico, 6wk). All can do AZ91D alloy.
DyCast is preferred for prototype - domestic simplifies quality audits. What's their MOQ for prototypes?
DyCast has no MOQ for proto tooling. T0 samples in 8 weeks from tooling release. $12k for soft tool. They need the 3D model by next Friday.
Approved. @U013 send the RFQ with the Parasolid from @U001. Let's get magnesium prototypes in hand for DVT comparison testing.
Weekly supplier scorecard: 94% on-time delivery this month. Two late shipments - both from the connector vendor (Molex). Escalating.
The Molex issue is becoming a pattern. @U017 set up a call with their regional sales manager. We need commits.
Arrow confirmed XC7A50T stock: 820 pcs available. PO for 60 pcs placed. Expected delivery: 5 business days. Crisis averted.
Excellent. @U011 please update the risk register - FPGA supply moved from red to green.
Updated. Also adding a note to qualify the XC7A50T as the primary going forward, with XC7A35T as fallback.
DVT BOM cost rollup complete. Per unit: $3,240 base + $14 TIM upgrade + $16 cable glands + $52 FPGA upgrade = $3,322 per unit. 15 units = $49,830.
Within the $55k budget with $5.2k contingency. That's about 10% - adequate for DVT.
The magnesium housing prototypes are separate - $12k tooling + ~$800/part for 3 test samples. That's an additional $14.4k from the NRE budget.
NRE budget has $25k remaining. $14.4k for mag housing exploration is approved. Go ahead.
Given the FPGA scare, I want second sources for all critical components by PVT. Priority: FPGA (done), LIDAR module, BLDC motors, battery cells.
LIDAR: Ouster OS0 is drop-in replacement for Velodyne. Need engineering eval. Motors: Oriental Motor has an equivalent. Battery: Samsung INR21700 as alt to LG.
Get samples of all three alternates. We'll qualify them in parallel with DVT testing.
Molex call done. They're allocating stock from their Asia warehouse. ETA 2 weeks for the connector backlog. They also offered 5% discount on next PO as goodwill.
Accept the discount, but I want a contractual on-time delivery guarantee for PVT quantities. Put it in the next PO terms.
DVT unit packaging spec needed. These are going to 3 test labs + 2 customer sites. Need to survive freight shipping (ISTA 3A equivalent).
Standard approach: custom foam inserts in a double-wall corrugated box. The robot weighs 18kg so we need the 275# burst strength boxes.
Get quotes for 25 packaging sets (15 robots + 10 spares for repack). Include tilt and shock indicators inside each box.
DyCast confirmed magnesium proto tool kickoff. T0 samples expected in 8 weeks. Cost: $11.8k for tool, $780 per part for 3 samples.
Velodyne LIDAR PO confirmed. 15 units shipping week of May 12. On track for DVT build schedule.
Quarterly business review with top 5 suppliers scheduled for next month. Focus: PVT volume commits and long-lead-time buffer stock agreements.
BOM frozen for DVT. All POs placed. Long-lead items on track. Next milestone: first article inspection in 4 weeks.
Motor 2 hit thermal shutdown during the 4-hour endurance run. Winding temp reached 158C, limit is 155C. Looking at the thermal interface between motor and chassis.
What TIM are you using? The Bergquist Gap Pad 5000S35 we spec'd is 3.5 W/mK. Should be sufficient.
That's what's on the BOM but I'm measuring actual thermal resistance and it's 2x what the datasheet says. Checking if the pad is making full contact.
Found it. The motor flange has a 0.3mm step that creates an air gap under the thermal pad. The pad can't conform to the step - it's too stiff.
Switch to Fujipoly XR-Um? It's softer (Shore 00-45 vs Shore 00-65) and conforms to surface irregularities. 5.0 W/mK conductivity too.
Good call. But the root cause is actually the chassis machining - that 0.3mm step shouldn't be there. @U001 can you check the chassis drawing tolerance?
Checked. The motor flange mounting face is spec'd at 0.1mm flatness. The step is a machining defect on this prototype. Production tooling won't have this issue.
OK so for DVT prototypes we need the softer TIM to handle surface variation. For production we can go back to the Gap Pad. Adding both to the BOM with a phase note.
Also flagging: this thermal issue affects all 4 motors, not just motor 2. The chassis mounting faces on all prototypes likely have the same machining variation.
Confirming - motor 4 showed elevated temps too (148C) during the same test. Was about to hit shutdown.
Battery pack temp during fast charge: 42C peak, 35C steady state. Spec limit is 45C. Adequate margin but worth monitoring as ambient temp increases.
What ambient are you testing at? If that's 22C lab temp, we'll be closer to limit in a 35C warehouse.
22C ambient. Good point - I'll add 35C ambient test to the DVT thermal validation matrix.
QA flagged cooling fan noise at 4200rpm. Measured 62dBA at 1m. Product spec is 55dBA max. Need fix before DVT.
That's the Sunon MF40201V2. We can drop to 3000rpm and still meet thermal targets if we improve the duct design. Working on CFD sim.
Need the fix validated before Thursday's DVT readiness review. Can you have sim results by then?
Yes, running overnight. Will post results tomorrow AM.
Fujipoly XR-Um test results: thermal resistance dropped from 2.8 C/W to 1.1 C/W with the softer pad. Motor winding temp now peaks at 138C - 17C margin to limit.
Excellent. That gives us enough margin even with the gearbox efficiency loss. Thermal is no longer a risk.
What's the cost delta? The Fujipoly is more expensive than the Bergquist per m2.
About $3.50 more per motor, $14 per robot. Worth it for 17C margin vs 13C with the stiffer pad.
Updated the BOM with Fujipoly for DVT builds. Added a note that production may revert to Bergquist if chassis machining tolerances are tighter.
CFD results for the revised fan duct: at 3000rpm we get 1.8 CFM through the heat sink. Required is 1.5 CFM for thermal compliance. 20% margin.
What's the noise level at 3000rpm?
Estimated 48dBA at 1m based on the fan curve. Well under the 55dBA spec. The duct redesign helped - better flow path means less turbulent noise.
Main PCB thermal scan: hottest component is the DC-DC converter at 78C. Rated to 105C. 27C margin is adequate.
The DC-DC is derated above 85C ambient. At 35C ambient the junction will be around 91C. Still 14C margin.
Good point. I'll add the derating curve to the thermal budget spreadsheet. MCU is at 62C, well within limits.
Thermal DVT test plan: ambient soak from -10C to 50C, thermal cycling -20C to 60C x 100 cycles, thermal shock per IEC 60068-2-14.
Thermal chamber is booked for weeks 13-14. I'll need 2 complete units for the full thermal validation suite.
Cold start test at -10C: system boots in 45 seconds. Battery delivers 92% of room-temp capacity. Motor controller needs 30s heater warm-up before operation.
The 30s heater warm-up is because the MOSFET Rds(on) increases at cold. Below -5C the current limit kicks in before the motor reaches full torque.
Can the warm-up run in parallel with boot? User sees boot time of 45s regardless, the heater just needs to finish before first move command.
Yes, heater starts at power-on. By the time boot and sensor init complete (~45s), the driver board is warm. Zero additional delay in practice.
Ordered 10x Fujipoly XR-Um thermal pads for DVT. 5-day lead time. Will have on hand before build.
CFD validation: measured airflow matches sim within 8%. Good correlation. The revised duct design is confirmed for DVT.
Thermal chamber calibration certificate received. ±0.5C accuracy from -40C to 150C. Ready for DVT.
Thermal changes frozen: Fujipoly TIM for DVT, revised fan duct at 3000rpm, 35C ambient added to test matrix. All validated.
